_metadata.description: Supplement timing and absorption interaction rules for optimal nutrient uptake

timing_rules.advice: Take iron and calcium at least 2 hours apart — calcium significantly reduces iron absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Calcium inhibits both non-heme and heme iron absorption in a dose-dependent manner (significant above 300 mg). The exact mechanism is not fully elucidated — the 1991 Hallberg study found the effect is not solely located in the GI tract and is independent of phytate binding. Clinically, separation by 2+ hours avoids the interaction.

sources.label: Hallberg L et al. — Calcium: effect of different amounts on nonheme- and heme-iron absorption in humans. Am J Clin Nutr. 1991
Hallberg L et al. — Calcium and iron absorption: mechanism of action and nutritional importance

timing_rules.advice: If you take higher-dose iron (about 25 mg or more), space it from zinc by a couple of hours — at those doses on an empty stomach they compete for the same transporter. Taking either with food blunts the effect.
timing_rules.mechanism: Both iron and zinc are divalent cations absorbed via DMT1; high molar ratios of one suppress the other's uptake in a dose-dependent manner. The competition is meaningful mainly at supplemental iron doses around 25 mg or higher taken fasting; food largely abolishes it.

sources.label: Kordas K, Stoltzfus RJ — New evidence of iron and zinc interplay at the enterocyte and neural tissues

timing_rules.advice: Avoid taking iron, calcium, and zinc together — all three compete for the same absorption pathway and significantly reduce each other's uptake.
timing_rules.mechanism: Divalent cation competition at DMT1 and related transporters; aggregate inhibition at the intestinal brush border is additive when all three minerals are present simultaneously.

sources.label: Linus Pauling Institute — Mineral Interactions

timing_rules.advice: Take iron and vitamin C together — vitamin C enhances non-heme iron absorption by 2 to 6 times.
timing_rules.mechanism: Ascorbic acid reduces ferric iron (Fe³⁺) to ferrous iron (Fe²⁺), the form transported by DMT1, and forms a soluble chelate that resists inhibition by phytate and tannins.

sources.label: Hallberg L et al. — The role of vitamin C in iron absorption

timing_rules.advice: Take calcium and vitamin D together — vitamin D is required for active calcium absorption in the small intestine.
timing_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D is essential for efficient calcium absorption. The 2011 IOM Dietary Reference Intakes report confirmed that adequate vitamin D status is required to meet calcium requirements. Taking them together ensures both are bioavailable during the absorption window.

sources.label: Ross AC, Manson JE, Abrams SA et al. — The 2011 IOM report on dietary reference intakes for calcium and vitamin D. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2011

timing_rules.advice: Take vitamins A, D, E, and K with a meal containing fat — these fat-soluble vitamins require dietary lipids for absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Fat-soluble vitamins are incorporated into mixed micelles formed from bile salts and dietary fatty acids; without fat in the GI lumen, micelle formation is insufficient and lymphatic absorption is markedly impaired.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin D Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take vitamin D with your largest or fattiest meal of the day for best absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) is a fat-soluble secosteroid; co-ingestion with fat increases micellar solubilization and lymphatic uptake, measurably raising serum 25(OH)D compared to fasted dosing.

sources.label: Linus Pauling Institute — Vitamin D

timing_rules.advice: Vitamin B12 and folate work synergistically and can be taken together at any time of day.
timing_rules.mechanism: Both vitamins are required for the methionine synthase reaction that converts homocysteine to methionine; B12 regenerates tetrahydrofolate from 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, making co-supplementation mutually supportive.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Folate Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take magnesium with food to ease the loose stools or stomach upset it can cause on an empty stomach.
timing_rules.mechanism: Unabsorbed magnesium salts are osmotically active in the gut and can cause diarrhea, especially in poorly-absorbed forms (oxide, citrate). Taking magnesium with a meal slows transit and eases this without meaningfully reducing absorption.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (forms, absorption, and GI tolerance)

timing_rules.advice: If you take magnesium for sleep, evening is a reasonable time — but mainly as a well-tolerated wind-down habit, not a proven sleep aid.
timing_rules.mechanism: Magnesium's effect on sleep is modest and seen mainly in people who are low in magnesium or have insomnia, and the evidence is limited and low-certainty. No trial has compared morning versus evening dosing, so taking it at night is a sensible routine rather than a proven timing effect — magnesium is well-tolerated in the evening and pairs naturally with a wind-down routine.

sources.label: Arab A, Rafie N, Amani R, Shirani F — The Role of Magnesium in Sleep Health: a Systematic Review of Available Literature (Biol Trace Elem Res 2023)
Mah J, Pitre T — Oral magnesium supplementation for insomnia in older adults: a Systematic Review & Meta-Analysis (BMC Complement Med Ther 2021)

timing_rules.advice: Take melatonin 30 to 60 minutes before your target bedtime for best effect on sleep onset.
timing_rules.mechanism: Exogenous melatonin binds MT1/MT2 receptors in the SCN to reinforce the circadian sleep-wake signal; the 30–60 min window aligns with endogenous dim-light melatonin onset and the descending phase of core body temperature.

sources.label: Arendt J & Skene DJ — Melatonin as a chronobiotic. Sleep Med Rev. 2005

timing_rules.advice: Take iron on an empty stomach for maximum absorption — if it causes stomach upset, take it with a small amount of food (but avoid dairy, tea, or coffee).
timing_rules.mechanism: Gastric acid in the fasted state reduces Fe³⁺ to the more soluble Fe²⁺ form; food (especially calcium, phytates, and polyphenols) in the GI lumen significantly inhibits non-heme iron uptake.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Iron Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

timing_rules.advice: Probiotic timing depends on formulation. Enteric-coated capsules can be taken any time. For standard probiotics, taking with a small meal may improve survival. Follow manufacturer guidance when available.
timing_rules.mechanism: The optimal timing for probiotics is debated. Fasted gastric pH is actually lower (more acidic, ~1.5-2.0) than postprandial pH (~3-5 due to food buffering). However, fasted gastric transit is faster, reducing total acid exposure time. Some evidence (Tompkins 2011, PMID 22146689) suggests probiotic survival is better WITH a meal containing fat or oatmeal due to acid buffering. Enteric-coated formulations bypass this concern. Net effect depends on strain, formulation, and meal composition.

sources.label: Tompkins TA et al. — The impact of meals on a probiotic during transit through a model of the human upper gastrointestinal tract

timing_rules.advice: Take CoQ10 with a meal containing fat — it is fat-soluble and absorption increases significantly with dietary lipids.
timing_rules.mechanism: Coenzyme Q10 is highly lipophilic; co-ingestion with fat enhances micellar solubilization in the GI lumen and facilitates chylomicron-mediated absorption into the lymphatic system.

sources.label: Linus Pauling Institute — Coenzyme Q10

timing_rules.advice: Take omega-3 fish oil with your largest meal of the day — fat in the meal enhances absorption and reduces fishy burps.
timing_rules.mechanism: EPA and DHA are long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids incorporated into chylomicrons during lipid digestion; dietary fat stimulates bile secretion and pancreatic lipase activity, both required for fatty acid emulsification and uptake.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Omega-3 Fatty Acids Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take turmeric or curcumin with a fatty meal and alongside black pepper (piperine) to dramatically increase absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Curcumin is hydrophobic with very low oral bioavailability; dietary fat promotes micellar solubilization, while piperine inhibits hepatic and intestinal glucuronidation, together increasing systemic curcumin exposure by up to 2,000%.

sources.label: Linus Pauling Institute — Curcumin

timing_rules.advice: Take fiber supplements at least 2 hours away from minerals like iron, calcium, and zinc — fiber binds minerals and reduces their absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Soluble fiber (psyllium, guar gum, inulin) forms a viscous gel in the GI lumen that chelates divalent and trivalent cations, physically trapping minerals and preventing contact with absorptive epithelium.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Iron Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

timing_rules.advice: Mix psyllium with at least 8 oz of liquid, and take other medications at least 3 hours away.
timing_rules.mechanism: Bulk-forming psyllium absorbs water and swells in the gut. Taking it dry or with too little fluid can cause choking, and the gel can delay absorption of some oral medications.

sources.label: MedlinePlus — Psyllium

timing_rules.advice: Take green tea (EGCG) and iron at least 2 hours apart — polyphenols in green tea strongly inhibit non-heme iron absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: EGCG and other catechins form insoluble iron-polyphenol complexes in the GI lumen; this chelation prevents DMT1-mediated ferrous iron uptake and can reduce iron absorption by up to 90% at typical tea-drinking doses.

sources.label: Zijp IM et al. — Effect of tea and other dietary factors on iron absorption

timing_rules.advice: Avoid taking iron with coffee — tannins in coffee can reduce iron absorption by 40% or more.
timing_rules.mechanism: Chlorogenic acid and tannins in coffee bind non-heme iron in the GI lumen forming poorly soluble complexes; the inhibitory effect is dose-dependent and persists for up to one hour after coffee consumption.

sources.label: Morck TA et al. — Inhibition of food iron absorption by coffee

timing_rules.advice: If taking thyroid medication, keep calcium, iron, and magnesium supplements at least 4 hours away — they significantly impair thyroid hormone absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Calcium, iron, and magnesium form insoluble complexes with levothyroxine (T4) in the GI tract, reducing systemic bioavailability by 20–40%; FDA labeling explicitly warns of this interaction.

sources.label: FDA Drugs@FDA — Levothyroxine sodium product labels

timing_rules.advice: Keep iron supplements at least 4 hours away from thyroid medication — iron markedly reduces thyroid hormone absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Ferrous iron directly binds levothyroxine in the GI lumen, forming insoluble ferrous-levothyroxine complexes that are not absorbed; this interaction is clinically significant even at standard supplement doses.

sources.label: Skelin M et al. — Factors Affecting Gastrointestinal Absorption of Levothyroxine: A Review

timing_rules.advice: If you use berberine long-term, space it from B12 and consider monitoring your levels. Berberine acts like metformin, which can lower B12 over time — berberine's own effect is plausible but not proven.
timing_rules.mechanism: Berberine may impair B12 absorption through metformin-like effects on ileal intrinsic factor-mediated transport and gut microbiome changes. The precise mechanism is not established — no controlled human trials have directly demonstrated this interaction. The analogy is drawn from metformin's well-documented B12 depletion effect (PMID 10977010, Bauman 2000). Berberine's effects on folate transport (via PCFT) are a separate pathway and do not explain B12 impairment.

sources.label: Bauman WA et al. — Increased intake of calcium reverses vitamin B12 malabsorption induced by metformin (the metformin analogy this berberine precaution is based on; no direct berberine trial exists)

timing_rules.advice: Take alpha-lipoic acid on an empty stomach — food reduces its peak plasma concentration.
timing_rules.mechanism: ALA is absorbed via active sodium-dependent transporter (SMVT); food in the GI tract slows gastric emptying and competes for transporter capacity, reducing Cmax by approximately 30–40% compared to fasted dosing.

sources.label: Linus Pauling Institute — Lipoic Acid

timing_rules.advice: High-dose vitamin E supplements (above 400 IU/day) may antagonize vitamin K — take them at different times and inform your healthcare provider if you are on anticoagulants.
timing_rules.mechanism: High-dose alpha-tocopherol inhibits vitamin K-dependent carboxylase activity and may deplete vitamin K by competing for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase, potentially impairing synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin E Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take quercetin and vitamin C together — vitamin C enhances quercetin absorption and both have complementary antioxidant activity.
timing_rules.mechanism: Ascorbic acid regenerates oxidized quercetin (quercetin semiquinone) back to the active reduced form, extending its antioxidant half-life; vitamin C also improves quercetin solubility in the GI lumen and may upregulate cellular uptake.

sources.label: Linus Pauling Institute — Flavonoids: Quercetin

timing_rules.advice: Take bromelain on an empty stomach for systemic anti-inflammatory effects, or with meals if you want it to aid protein digestion.
timing_rules.mechanism: In the fasted state, bromelain is absorbed intact through intestinal epithelium into systemic circulation to exert anti-inflammatory and fibrinolytic effects; with food, its proteolytic activity is consumed in GI digestion before systemic absorption can occur.

sources.label: Castell JV et al. — Intestinal absorption of undegraded proteins in men: presence of bromelain in plasma after oral intake

timing_rules.advice: Take ashwagandha with food to minimize gastrointestinal discomfort — most GI side effects occur when taken on an empty stomach.
timing_rules.mechanism: Withanolides and other steroidal lactones in ashwagandha root extract can irritate gastric mucosa in susceptible individuals; food delays gastric emptying, dilutes the extract, and buffers mucosal contact.

sources.label: NCCIH — Ashwagandha: Usefulness and Safety

timing_rules.advice: Take L-theanine and caffeine together for synergistic focus and reduced jitteriness — the combination is well-supported by clinical research.
timing_rules.mechanism: L-theanine promotes alpha brain wave activity and modulates GABA/glutamate signaling, blunting caffeine's sympathomimetic side effects while preserving and enhancing its pro-attentional effects via adenosine antagonism.

sources.label: Owen GN et al. — The combined effects of L-theanine and caffeine on cognitive performance

timing_rules.advice: Maintain a consistent daily intake of vitamin K if you take warfarin — sudden changes in vitamin K intake can destabilize your INR and change your clotting status.
timing_rules.mechanism: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1); fluctuating vitamin K intake directly competes with this mechanism. Consistency, not avoidance, is the clinical recommendation.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin K Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take vitamin E with a fat-containing meal — as a fat-soluble vitamin, it requires dietary lipids for proper absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Alpha-tocopherol is incorporated into mixed micelles in the GI lumen alongside dietary fatty acids and bile salts; micellar solubilization is the rate-limiting step for its lymphatic uptake.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin E Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take vitamin K with a meal containing fat — it is fat-soluble and absorption is minimal without dietary lipids.
timing_rules.mechanism: Phylloquinone (K1) and menaquinones (K2) are fat-soluble; co-ingestion with fat significantly increases their micellar incorporation and subsequent chylomicron-mediated absorption from the small intestine.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin K Fact Sheet

timing_rules.advice: Take calcium supplements at least 4 hours away from thyroid medication — calcium chelates thyroid hormone and reduces its absorption.
timing_rules.mechanism: Calcium carbonate and calcium citrate both form insoluble complexes with levothyroxine in the stomach at physiologic pH; this interaction is well-documented in pharmacokinetic studies and is included in official FDA prescribing information.

sources.label: Singh N et al. — The acute effect of calcium carbonate on the intestinal absorption of levothyroxine

timing_rules.advice: Take zinc with a small amount of food to prevent nausea — zinc on an empty stomach commonly causes GI upset.
timing_rules.mechanism: Zinc salts (sulfate, gluconate, bisglycinate) can irritate the gastric mucosa and trigger nausea via local zinc ion-induced prostaglandin release; a small amount of food buffers this effect without significantly impairing overall zinc bioavailability.

sources.label: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Zinc Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

timing_rules.advice: Take levothyroxine at least 60 minutes before coffee — espresso reduces absorption by up to 36%.
timing_rules.mechanism: Coffee (especially espresso) reduces levothyroxine absorption by 25-36% when taken concurrently. The mechanism involves altered intestinal motility and pH changes from coffee's chlorogenic acids and other compounds that interfere with T4 dissolution and absorption in the proximal small intestine.

sources.label: Benvenga S et al. — Altered intestinal absorption of L-thyroxine caused by coffee. Thyroid. 2008

timing_rules.advice: Take calcium carbonate with meals — it requires stomach acid to dissolve. Calcium citrate can be taken without food.
timing_rules.mechanism: Calcium carbonate requires gastric acid to dissolve and release ionized calcium for absorption. In achlorhydric or fasted conditions, dissolution is markedly impaired. Calcium citrate is pre-solubilized and does not require acid, making it suitable for fasting or for patients on acid-suppressing medications (PPIs, H2 blockers).

sources.label: Recker RR — Calcium absorption and achlorhydria. N Engl J Med. 1985
NIH ODS — Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

timing_rules.advice: Take levothyroxine at least 4 hours apart from soy protein or soy isoflavone supplements.
timing_rules.mechanism: Soy protein and soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) can reduce levothyroxine absorption and may also inhibit thyroid peroxidase, compounding the effect in hypothyroid patients. The FDA prescribing information for levothyroxine warns about concurrent soy intake.

sources.label: Messina M & Redmond G — Effects of soy protein and soybean isoflavones on thyroid function. Thyroid. 2006
